title-group.article-title: Turning the Near-Infrared Shielding Performance of Erbium/Antimony-Codoped Tin Oxide Nanomaterials Derived via a Sonochemically Assisted Method

article-meta.aff: a Department of Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, School of Integrated Innovative Technology, King Mongkut’s Institute of Technology Ladkrabang, Bangkok 10520, Thailand
b Department of Physics, Sri Venkateswara University, Tirupati 517 502, India
c Department of Physics, Amrita School of Engineering, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham, Amaravati, Andhra Pradesh 522 502, India

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Authors. Published by American Chemical Society

license.license-p: This article is licensed under CC-BY-NC-ND 4.0

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Energy consumption is one of the most important concerning issues of environmental problems especially the global warming phenomenon, with energy usage being the main cause. To reduce the energy used for cooling systems, development of material for smart buildings for near-infrared (NIR) protection is a challenge of material society. Metal oxides including tin oxide (SnO2) with an antimony (Sb) dopant are a candidate owing to their NIR shielding property. However, the upgrading of NIR absorption performance with rare-earth doping due to the specific property of rare-earth elements especially erbium (Er) shows strong absorption of the near-infrared region. This article synthesized Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 nanoparticles by sonochemical and thermal treatment processes at various Er dopant concentrations with a Sb dopant concentration stabilized at 5%. Crystallinity and morphology exhibited the tetragonal phase of SnO2 with a sphere-like nanostructure with an approximately average size of 10 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to evaluate the chemical oxidation state of all elements, which verified the Er3+ and Sb5+ oxidation states of dopant ions, and oxygen vacancy observation led to the NIR absorption improvement.

sec.p: Received 2026 Jan 21; Accepted 2026 Mar 13; Revised 2026 Mar 12; Collection date 2026 Apr 7.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p:
In recent years, the accelerating effects of global warming and rising energy demands have become urgent environmental challenges worldwide. Among all domestic appliances requiring energy, household cooling systems represent a major energy consumer, as they are essential for maintaining comfortable indoor environments in increasingly warmer climates. , Therefore, development of energy-efficient cooling strategies is a critically important issue. One promising approach is reduction of the internal heat load of buildings through the integration of advanced thermal insulator materials, particularly in the design of smart building components such as energy-saving windows, coatings, and thin-film technology. Smart materials capable of minimizing heat transfer by shielding of near-infrared (NIR) radiation while maintaining visible-light transparency are being extensively researched for this purpose. NIR radiation, which constitutes a significant component of solar energy, can easily penetrate traditional glass and translucent materials, leading to unwanted heat accumulation inside of the buildings. Thus, developing efficient NIR shielding materials is a key target in modern energy-saving and passive cooling technologies. A wide range of materials including sulfide-based compounds, tungsten bronze derivatives, and various metal oxides have been investigated for this application due to their optical tunability and thermal stability. , Among various candidate materials, tin oxide (SnO2), a wide-band gap semiconductor (∼3.6 eV), stands out due to its excellent optical and electronic properties. It has been widely used in gas sensors, transparent electrodes, photocatalysts, and optoelectronic devices. , However, intrinsic SnO2 primarily absorbs ultraviolet light and lacks significant NIR shielding capabilities. To address this limitation, doping SnO2 with metal ions such as antimony (Sb) has been shown to induce localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), enabling strong NIR absorption. Antimony-doped tin oxide (ATO) nanoparticles have thus emerged as effective NIR shielding materials with additional conductivity and stability benefits. Various synthesis techniques have been explored for ATO nanoparticles, including the coprecipitation method, hydrothermal synthesis, and the sonochemical process. Among them, the sonochemical approach offers distinct advantages in producing fine, uniform nanoparticles through high-energy acoustic cavitation, which enhances the reaction kinetics and particle dispersion. To further enhance NIR shielding performance, recent research has turned to codoping strategies, particularly with rare-earth elements. Rare-earth dopants possess unique 4f electron configurations and discrete energy levels, which allow them to interact with incident light in specialized ways, including up-conversion, down-shifting, and NIR absorption. −
Among these rare-earth elements, erbium (Er) is a promising candidate due to its well-known luminescence and NIR absorption bands arising from intra-4f transitions. When introduced into suitable host lattices, erbium can modulate the electronic structure and enhance plasmonic and shielding effects. ,

In this study, we report the synthesis of erbium and antimony-codoped tin oxide (Er/Sb-codoped SnO2) nanoparticles via a sonochemically assisted method followed by controlled thermal treatment. By systematically varying Er dopant concentrations (0.1–5 mol %), we investigate the resulting changes in structural, morphological, optical, and NIR shielding properties. Advanced characterization techniques, including X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), are employed to analyze the chemical bonding states and carrier concentrations of the doped systems. Finally, the practical thermal shielding performance of the materials is evaluated using a model test box setup, where films coated with the synthesized nanoparticles are exposed to NIR radiation. This research provides new insight into the design of multifunctional, energy-efficient coatings for smart building applications.

sec.title: Materials
sec.p: All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The starting materials were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, including tin­(IV) chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O), antimony­(III) chloride (SbCl3), and erbium­(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Er­(NO3)3·5 H2O). Absolute ethanol (EtOH) was sourced from Merck as the chemical solvent, and ammonia solution (NH3) was supplied by RCI Labscan Limited for pH adjustment.

sec.title: Synthesis of Er/Sb-Codoped SnO2 Nanoparticles
sec.p: Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 nanoparticles were synthesized via a sonochemically assisted sol–gel process followed by thermal treatment. Initially, SnCl4·5H2O was dissolved in absolute ethanol under continuous stirring to form a clear solution. A fixed amount of SbCl3 corresponding to 5 mol % Sb and varying amounts of Er­(NO3)3·5 H2O (0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, and 5 mol %) were separately dissolved in ethanol. These dopant precursor solutions were then added to the Sn precursor solution, and the mixture was stirred thoroughly. Acetylacetone was introduced as a reducing agent, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 9 by using ammonia. The resulting solution was subjected to ultrasonic irradiation (20 kHz, 750 W) for 30 min using a sonochemical probe to promote nanoparticle formation. The resulting precipitate was washed repeatedly with deionized (DI) water until neutral pH (≈7) was reached, and then dried at 100 °C for 24 h. Finally, all dried samples were thermally treated at 400 °C for 2 h to obtain crystalline Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 powders. As a reference, pristine SnO2 nanoparticles were synthesized fusing the same procedure but without the addition of dopant precursors.

sec.title: Preparation of Thin Films
sec.p: Thin films were fabricated by using the doctor blade coating technique. A binder solution was prepared by dissolving 10 wt % poly­(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) in DI water. A dispersion was made by adding 0.01 g of each synthesized powder sample into 10 mL of the PVA solution, followed by stirring for 2 h to ensure uniform mixing. The homogeneous slurry was coated onto precleaned glass substrates (5 × 5 cm2) and dried in an oven at 100 °C for 10 min to evaporate residual solvent and moisture.

sec.title: Crystal Structure and Morphology
sec.p: X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by using a Rigaku SmartLab diffractometer to determine the crystal structure. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100 Plus) was employed to observe the surface morphology and lattice parameters. Optical properties were observed by measuring UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) using a Hitachi UH4150 spectrophotometer to analyze light absorption and reflectance characteristics. For surface chemistry, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, JEOL JPS-9030) with a magnesium (Mg) X-ray source was used to investigate the surface chemical composition and oxidation states of the constituent elements. Furthermore, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMX Micro) spectroscopy was used to detect unpaired electrons and assess the presence of oxygen vacancies in the doped and undoped samples. For thermal insulation testing, the thermal shielding properties of the coated films were evaluated by monitoring the temperature inside a model chamber covered with the film-coated glass substrates (5 × 5 cm2). The system was exposed to IR light (intensity ∼1000 lx, measured using a lx meter), and the internal temperature changes were recorded to assess thermal insulation performance.

sec.title: Crystalline Structure
sec.p: The crystalline structure of undoped and doped SnO2 specimens was investigated by the X-ray diffraction technique (XRD), as illustrated in Figure . First, the pristine SnO2 sample revealed the main diffraction positions located at 2o̷ = 26.7, 33.9, and 51.9° corresponding to the tetragonal structure of cassiterite SnO2 with planes (110), (101), and (211), respectively [JCPDS No. 01-075-9493]. After doping with 5 mol % of Sb into the SnO2 host material, the main resulting peak showed a slight shift to a higher-angle position due to the smaller ionic ratio size of Sb5+ dopant ions (0.60 Å), affecting the lattice spacing of SnO2. It means that the smaller dopant ion can cause lattice distortion, e.g., substitutional, interstitial, and vacant. , For codopants, the increase in their Er concentration still showed a peak shift to a higher angle position, showing that the ionic ratio of Er3+ is larger (0.89 Å) than that of Sn4+ ions (0.69 Å). The results suggested that the Er dopant could not be more affected by lattice spacing. Meanwhile, the increase in Er dopant concentration shows a broadening of the XRD peak since doping ions exhibited a disordered formation of SnO2, as expressed by the crystalline size calculated using Scherrer’s equation, showing a decrease in crystalline size when the Er dopant concentration increased (Table ). The results show that the decrease of crystalline size after the increase of the Er dopant concentration from 38 to 26 Å led to the formation of oxygen vacancies in the SnO2 matrix, which correlated with the grain boundary defects and retardation of the grain migration that inhibited the growth of the crystal. Moreover, the increase of the Er ion dopant exhibited a slight change of the lattice parameters, as depicted in Table , since the Er ion dopant affected the lattice distortion of the crystal, which correlated with the shift of the diffractogram fingerprint of SnO2.

caption.p: X-ray diffractogram spectra of pure SnO2, 5%Sb-doped SnO2, and Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 with different Er-containing samples.

caption.title: Calculation of the Lattice Parameters, Crystalline Size, and Cell Volume for Er/Sb-Codoped SnO2 Nanoparticles

sec.title: Optical Properties
sec.p: The optical properties of Er/Yb-codoped SnO2 nanoparticles were analyzed using the diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) technique, as illustrated in Figure . The pristine SnO2 sample exhibited strong absorption in the UV region, which corresponds to the intrinsic wide band gap of SnO2. In contrast, the reflectance spectrum showed high reflectance values up to 80% in the visible region and around 50–60% in the near-infrared (NIR) region. In the case of single doping with 5 mol % Sb, the optical properties changed significantly.

caption.p: Diffuse reflectance spectra of Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 at varied Er dopant concentrations (0.1–5 mol %) and fixed Sb dopant concentration (5 mol %).

sec.p: The reflectance dropped drastically, especially in the NIR region, where the reflectance approached 0%, indicating strong absorption. This suggests that Sb doping enhances free carrier absorption in the NIR region due to the introduction of donor states and oxygen vacancies. Similarly, Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 nanoparticles exhibited comparable behavior, with strong NIR absorption relative to intrinsic SnO2, further confirming the synergistic effect of codoping. As the Er dopant concentration increased, the reflectance spectra showed a noticeable increase in the visible-light reflectance. This implies that Er incorporation affects the optical response of SnO2, particularly by contributing to enhanced NIR absorption through localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects associated with oxygen vacancy-induced free carriers. Moreover, at the highest Er doping concentration of 5 mol %, a distinct absorption peak appeared at approximately 532 nm. This absorption band is attributed to electronic transitions from the Er3+ ground state 4I15/2 to the excited state 2H11/2, confirming the successful incorporation of Er ions into the SnO2 lattice. , These results collectively indicate that Er/Sb codoping in SnO2 nanoparticles significantly modifies their optical properties, especially enhancing NIR absorption, which is beneficial for thermal shielding and optoelectronic applications.

sec.title: Surface Morphology
sec.p:
The surface morphology and crystallographic features of the synthesized powder samples were investigated by using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). As shown in Figure , the HRTEM micrographs reveal clear lattice fringes, and the interplanar spacing (d-spacing) was measured using ImageJ software. The measured d-spacing values were approximately 3.2–3.3 Å, which closely correspond to the (110) lattice planes of tetragonal SnO2. Upon increasing the erbium (Er) dopant concentration, a slight reduction in the d-spacing was observed. This change is attributed to the introduction of structural defects caused by the incorporation of Er3+ ions into the SnO2 lattice, either by substitution of Sn4+ ions or by occupying interstitial sites. The ionic radius of Er3+ (0.89 Å) is larger than that of Sn4+ (0.69 Å), which can locally distort the lattice structure. However, the overall lattice variation depends on the dopant concentration ratio in the codoped system and typically follows a weighted average of the individual doping effects. According to the X-ray diffraction (XRD) results, a slight shift of diffraction peaks toward higher angles is shown, indicating a decrease in lattice spacing (lattice contraction), as shown by the change of cell volume in Table . This suggests that when the Sb dopant concentration is higher than that of Er, Sb5+ ions play a dominant role in the lattice modification, leading to the contraction of the SnO2 lattice, as shown in the XRD patterns. Moreover, when the Sb dopant concentration is significantly higher than the Er3+ concentration (typically exceeding 3–5 mol %), the cumulative contraction effect induced by Sb5+ substitution and the associated oxygen vacancies can outweigh the lattice expansion effect caused by Er3+ incorporation. This implies that superior Er codopant concentrations may reduce the formation of oxygen vacancies, which perform as free carriers responsible for NIR shielding. This behavior is consistent with the EPR results, where variations in signal intensity correspond to changes in free carrier concentration. ,−
Therefore, the HRTEM analysis provides direct nanoscale evidence of the dopant-induced lattice modification, confirming that the codoping of Er and Sb ions effectively alters the crystal structure of the SnO2 host material.

caption.p: HRTEM micrographs of (a) SnO2 (b) 5%Sb-doped SnO2 and (c–g) 0.1–5% Er/5%Sb-codoped SnO2 nanoparticles. (h) TEM micrograph of 1%Er/5%Sb-codoped SnO2 nanoparticles.

sec.title: Surface Chemical Analysis
sec.p: To investigate the effect of Er/Sb dopant ions on SnO2 nanoparticles, the chemical oxidation states of the elements were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectra of Sn 3d, Sb 3d, O 1s, and Er 4f for Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 samples are illustrated in Figure . The primary chemical state of Sn in all specimens was found to be Sn4+, with binding energies at 486 and 494.4 eV, corresponding to Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2, respectively, as shown in Figure a for the 1%Er/5%Sb-codoped SnO2 sample. Furthermore, the O 1s spectra of the SnO2 samples with 5% Sb single doping and various Er codopant concentrations are shown in Figure c. The deconvoluted spectrum reveals two peaks: one at 531 eV corresponding to the O 1s spin orbit of lattice oxygen and another at 529 eV corresponding to the Sb5+ oxidation state. In the case of 5 mol % Sb single doping, the O 1s spectrum reveals peaks associated with both lattice oxygen and oxygen vacancies, indicating that Sb doping effectively induces oxygen vacancy formation. This can be attributed to the substitution of Sb5+ at Sn4+ sites, which leads to charge imbalance and generates unpaired electrons on the nanoparticle surface, resulting in oxygen vacancies. ,, For rare-earth doping, the codoped sample containing 0.1 mol % Er with 5 mol % Sb showed a similar XPS pattern, especially the Sb5+ peak at 529 eV, corresponding to Sb 3d3/2. Additionally, the oxygen vacancy-related O 1s peak was still observed at 531 eV, confirming the retention of oxygen defects in the SnO2 host lattice. As the Er dopant concentration increased (while maintaining at 5 mol % of Sb), the XPS spectra in Figure c showed a notable increase in oxygen species, including surface-adsorbed oxygen and oxygen vacancies. This increase is attributed to the enhanced electron density surrounding Sn4+ ions caused by Er3+ incorporation. The elevated electron density reduces O2– ions in the lattice, promoting nonstoichiometry and thereby generating more oxygen vacancies. These vacancies enhance the adsorption capacity for oxygen species and contribute to maintaining charge equilibrium in the lattice. , Additionally, the narrow-scan spectrum of Er ions, as shown in Figure b, confirms the presence of Er3+ with a 4d3/2 spin orbital at a binding energy of 170.5 eV. It confirmed that the introduction of Er/Sb codopant ions significantly promotes oxygen vacancy formation in the SnO2 lattice. This structural modification contributes directly to enhanced NIR absorption performance, making Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 a promising material for thermal shielding and optoelectronic applications.

caption.p: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic spectra of (a) Sn 3d and (b) Er 4d orbitals of the 1%Er/5%Sb-codoped SnO2 sample and (c) Sb 3d with O 1s spectra of all samples with different 0–5 mol % of Er dopant concentrations (c–h).

sec.title: Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) Analysis
sec.p: To explore the oxygen vacancy generation, electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopic (EPR) measurement was used to verify the unpaired electrons in terms of free carriers and radicals. Figure represents the Gaussian derivative fitting result of the EPR spectra showing the relation of magnetic field (mT) and the EPR dispersion derivative. EPR measurements were performed at a microwave frequency of 9.8 GHz under an applied external magnetic field. EPR is a powerful technique used to investigate the behavior of unpaired electrons through their energy level transitions, which follows the relationship ΔE = gμB B 0, where g is the electron g-factor, μB is the Bohr magneton, and B 0 is the external magnetic field. As shown in Figure , the EPR spectra exhibit resonance signals at 336.5, 336.5, 336.2, 339.5, 336.1, 335.1, and 334.6 mT for pure SnO2, 5%Sb-SnO2, 0.1%Er/5%Sb-SnO2, 0.5%Er/5%Sb-SnO2, 1%Er/5%Sb-SnO2, 3%Er/5%Sb-SnO2, and 5%Er/5%Sb-SnO2 samples, respectively. The observation of variation in peak position and signal intensity is attributed to the increasing number of defects particularly oxygen vacancies as the Er codopant concentration increases. The optimum Er/Sb codoping concentration was observed at 1 mol % Er with 5 mol % Sb, which showed the highest EPR signal intensity. This indicates a maximum generation of unpaired electrons or defect sites at this composition. Additionally, the narrow and sharp EPR spectrum of the 1%Er/5%Sb-SnO2 sample suggests strong exchange interactions and delocalization of unpaired electrons within the conjugated chemical bonding network. , Therefore, the EPR analysis confirms the presence of oxygen vacancy defects and free electrons in Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 samples. The increased free electron density associated with codoping effectively contributes to the enhancement of near-infrared (NIR) absorption performance.

caption.p: EPR spectroscopic measurement of bare SnO2, singly 5%Sb-doped SnO2, and Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 with different Er loading concentrations.

sec.title: Electrical Resistivity
sec.p: According to the EPR results that contributed to the existing oxygen vacancy defect, electrical conductivity observation was used to confirm the reason for the Er/Sn-codoped SnO2 samples by using 4-point probe measurement. First, as-prepared Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 powder samples were pressed by using a clod sintering machine with a pressure of 1500 psi for 5 min to obtain pellet samples, followed by electrical resistivity measurement. The pristine SnO2 sample showed the highest resistivity at 3.082 MΩ, while the sample singly doped with Sb showed a lower resistivity of about 2.249 MΩ. Furthermore, the Er/Sb codopant demonstrated the slight change of electrical resistivities of 2.237, 2.257, 2.242, 2.240, and 3.075 for 0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, and 5%Er/5%Sb codopant concentrations, respectively. The decrease in electrical resistivity after single doping with the 5% Sb concentration is explained by the increase in free carrier concentration, as Sb5+ ions incorporated at the Sn4+ sites behave as donor ions. In the codoping system, at lower Er3+ concentrations (with dominant Sb5+ doping), the system remains donor-dominated from Sb5+ behaving as the donor, leading to high carrier density and strong NIR shielding via free carrier absorption. However, with the Er3+ at higher content playing as the acceptor, increased acceptor-type defects and ionized impurity scattering may reduce carrier mobility and partially compensate for Sb-induced electrons. Consequently, excessive Er doping can decrease the effective plasmonic absorption in the NIR region. This behavior aligns with EPR observations related to oxygen vacancy variations. In addition, the decrease in crystalline size with increasing Er3+ codopant concentration results in an expanded grain boundary region, which affects carrier migration between crystals. This observation correlates with the X-ray diffraction results, which show peak broadening with increasing Er codopant concentration. These results can support the oxygen vacancy generation of Er/Sb codopant SnO2 nanoparticles, which exhibited an NIR shielding property.

sec.title: Thermal Insulation Property
sec.p: Considering the thermal shielding property, optical transmission is a crucial factor for evaluation. The transmittance of the prepared films was analyzed, as illustrated in Figure b. All samples exhibited a high visible-light transmission and significant absorption in the near-infrared (NIR) region. Typically, a bare borosilicate glass substrate demonstrated over 90% transmittance in the visible region, with a slight decrease in the NIR range due to the inherent properties of borosilicate glass. Moreover, all tested films showed similar transmission spectra, characterized by high transmittance in the visible range and a notable reduction in the NIR region. To better quantify this effect, the difference in transmittance (ΔT) between the visible and NIR regions was calculated using the following equations:

caption.p: (a) Thermal shielding performance set up by apparatus. (b) Optical transmission of all films. (c) Temperature measurement inside of the testing box covered with testing films under 1500 lx of IR lamp exposure (inset temperature testing for superior condition with error bar). (d) Repeatability testing of the 1%Er/5%Sb-SnO2 film.

sec.p: The calculated ΔT values for all of the film samples are summarized in Table . The ΔT value reflects the difference in transmission between the visible and NIR regions, which correlates with the thermal shielding capability of the material. To verify the thermal shielding property, the thermal insulation performance of the films was assessed by measuring the temperature inside a testing box, which was set up with apparatus, as illustrated in Figure a . The box was covered with the film-coated glass substrates (5 × 5 cm2) and exposed to an infrared (IR) lamp (1500 lx). The results, shown in Figure b, reveal that the uncovered box (air) and the bare glass substrate reached the highest internal temperature of approximately 39 °C after 80 min of IR exposure. A film coated with poly­(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) showed a slightly reduced temperature of around 37 °C, attributed to the IR-absorbing functional groups in the PVA matrix. In comparison, the pure SnO2 film showed a similar high temperature of 39 °C. However, when doped with 5% Sb, the SnO2 film exhibited a lower internal temperature of approximately 36 °C. Introducing a small amount of Er codopant (0.1%) led to a further decrease in temperature to around 37 °C. This improvement is associated with the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect caused by free electrons, which arise from oxygen vacancy defects induced by Sb doping. , As the Er codoping concentration increased, a consistent decrease in temperature was observed after 80 min of IR exposure. The incorporation of Er3+ ions help to maintain charge neutrality within the SnO2 lattice, enhancing the electron density around Sn4+ sites and inducing nonstoichiometry. This, in turn, promotes the formation of oxygen vacancies, as supported by XPS analysis. The optimal codoping condition was found to be 1%Er/5%Sb, which exhibited the most effective thermal shielding, reducing the temperature to approximately 35 °C. Moreover, the stability of the sample is an important factor for smart building applications. The stability of the optimized 1%Er/5%Sb-codoped SnO2 film was evaluated through repeated testing cycles. The thermal shielding performance of the film was examined under IR lamp exposure for 80 min per cycle, and the results are presented in Figure d. The film maintained a consistent internal temperature of approximately 31 °C inside the testing box over five consecutive cycles. This result confirms that the 1%Er/5%Sb-codoped SnO2 material synthesized via the sonochemically assisted method exhibits good thermal stability and durability. Therefore, it can be considered a promising candidate material for smart building applications.

caption.title: Resistivity of Powder Samples and Optical Property of Films

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In this study, Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 nanoparticles successfully synthesized sonochemically assisted and thermal treatment techniques. The influence of the Er dopant concentration on the structural, optical, electrical, and thermal shielding properties was systematically investigated. Structural analysis confirmed the retention of the tetragonal SnO2 phase, with a slight lattice contraction due to Er incorporation, as evidenced by XRD and HRTEM results. XPS and EPR analyses revealed enhanced oxygen vacancy formation associated with Er and Sb codoping, which contributed to increased free carrier concentration. Optical studies showed strong UV absorption and significant suppression of NIR reflectance, particularly at optimal codoping levels, due to localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effects. A notable absorption peak at 532 nm confirmed the presence of Er3+ dopant ions. Thermal shielding tests demonstrated a reduction in internal temperature under IR exposure, with the best performance achieved at 1 mol % Er and 5 mol % Sb codoping, corresponding to enhanced NIR absorption capability. These findings indicate that Er/Sb-codoped SnO2 is a promising multifunctional material for energy-efficient glazing, smart windows, and NIR shielding applications, offering an effective strategy for managing solar heat gain in buildings.

sec.title: Acknowledgments
sec.p: The authors would like to thank the Advanced Technology Testing and Analysis Center (ATTAC) of School of Integrated Innovative Technology (SiiTec), King Mongkut’s Institute of Technology Ladkrabang (KMITL), and Kyoto University under the Japan-Asian Science Technology and Innovation Platform (JASTIP) program for supporting characterization facilities.

Thanaphon Kansaard: writingoriginal draft, methodology, data curation, and conceptualization. Tanisara Noinonmueng: methodology, data curation, and validation. C.K. Jayasankar: investigation. Wanichaya Mekprasart: investigation and visualization. Kanokthip Boonyarattanakalin: formal analysis, investigation, and visualization. Wisanu Pecharapa: writingreview and editing, funding acquisition, supervision, and project administration.

This research has received funding support from the National Science Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) via the Program Management Unit for Human Resources & Institutional Development, Research and Innovation (Grant No. B13F680084).
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
